text: The surgeon demonstrates the placement of standard laparoscopic ports and initiates dissection. The left and right middle colic arteries are clearly visible, and the surgeon carefully dissects each artery, applying clips to secure them.

The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the vessels, accessing the lesser sac. Gentle dissection reveals the posterior aspect of the stomach, allowing division of the mesocolon and reaching the right middle colic vessels. The greater omentum is separated from the colon.
The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the completed dissection from below with visible clips applied to the dissected vessels.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels using a medial to lateral dissection approach. The vessels are dissected free, clips are applied, and the vessel is subsequently divided.

The surgeon visualizes the duodenum below and performs a full dissection over its surface, tracing its curve from the first to the second part. The anatomical relations are visible, and the surgeon proceeds with lateral to medial exposure of the right hemicolon.

The surgeon continues the dissection to connect with the previously performed medial to lateral dissection. The right colic artery is visible, with the view achieved by suspending the windows created in the ileocolic region and from the division of the middle colic vessels.

The surgeon frees the descending colon and sigmoid from a lateral to medial approach, mobilizing the colon up to the splenic flexure. The splenic flexure is completely mobilized, liberating the greater omentum from the colon. The stomach is visible at the top of the field, providing a clear view of the pancreas. The left hemicolon and mesocolon are exposed, with individual vessels identified and divided using an energy device.

The surgeon places endoclips approximately five centimeters before the rectosigmoid junction to mark the desired anastomosis location. Segmental sigmoid arteries are clipped and divided using an energy device to prevent leaving a redundant loop of sigmoid colon in the pelvis.
The surgeon transects the distal ileum on the right side of the dissection, preparing to extract the colon.

The surgeon makes a mini Pfannenstiel incision and inserts a wound protector. The colon is delivered through the incision, and the ileum is exteriorized. Interrupted sutures using 3-0 Vicryl are placed in a side-to-side configuration to stabilize the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates enterotomies in both the ileum and colon, inserting a linear stapling device. Forceps are used to prevent knots from catching in the staple line, ensuring the anti-mesenteric borders are stapled. A transverse stapler is applied across the ileum and colon to complete the side-to-side anastomosis configuration.
The surgeon transfixes any bleeding points with a 3-0 Vicryl suture. The staple line is buried using 3-0 Vicryl sutures, and a crotch stitch is placed to stabilize the anastomosis.

During re-inspection, the staple line is visible along with the terminal ileum and the loops leading to it. The surgeon lifts the small bowel mesentery, tracing it in a straight, non-twisted line back to the duodenum.
